I researched this thoroughly (n8n, OpenClaw, MCP servers, and the major agent frameworks). Here's everything, laid out as options for you to choose from. Note: "openclaw" is a real tool — OpenClaw, a free, open-source, locally-run personal AI agent that went viral in late 2025/2026. ## What the pipeline needs to do (common to every option) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Google Doc
problem statement + IO constraints] --> B[Ingest / read doc] B --> C[Parse and normalize sections] C --> D[Requirement analysis
functional, non-functional, inputs, outputs, constraints, edge cases, assumptions] D --> E[Problem tree generation
hierarchical decomposition] E --> F[Output
Markdown + Mermaid / JSON / write back to Doc] ``` The "problem tree" output would be a hierarchical decomposition: root problem → sub-problems → requirements → constraints/inputs/outputs → edge cases. Renderable as a Mermaid diagram, a Markdown outline, or structured JSON. ## How to connect to the Google Doc (a sub-decision, applies to all options) - Official Google Drive remote MCP server — `https://drivemcp.googleapis.com/mcp/v1`, HTTP transport, OAuth 2.0. Tools: `search_files`, `read_file_content`. Most legit, Google-maintained. - Community Google Docs MCP servers — e.g. `petergarety/gdrive-mcp` (has `get_document_text`, `get_document_headings`, `get_content_under_heading`), `ugiordan/google-docs-mcp-server`, `Prakul95/mcp-server-google-docs`. Work in Cursor/Claude via stdio. - Google Docs API directly — OAuth or service account, `documents.get`. Most control, more setup. - Simplest hack — File → Share → Publish to web (or download as `.txt`) and feed the text/URL. Zero auth, good for a one-off. ## The build approaches (pick one) ### Option A — Cursor-native (MCP + this agent, optionally a reusable Skill) — Recommended for "here in cursor" - Add a Google Docs MCP server to Cursor → I read the doc directly → produce the requirement analysis + problem tree as Markdown/Mermaid in your repo. - Wrap it as a reusable Cursor Skill / command so it's one click each time; make it "autonomous"/recurring via Cursor Automations, hooks, or the `/loop` skill. - Pros: lowest effort, lives where you already work, no separate infra, version-controlled output. Cons: tied to Cursor; "autonomy" is light (triggered/scheduled rather than always-on). ### Option B — n8n visual workflow - Trigger (schedule/webhook/chat) → Google Docs node → AI Agent node (LangChain, Tools Agent) → Structured Output Parser → write result to a new Doc / Slack / file. - Pros: self-hostable, great for scheduled autonomy, mostly no-code, easy to add human-approval gates. Cons: separate service to run/host. ### Option C — Python service with LangGraph or CrewAI — Recommended if you want a real autonomous product - LangGraph: deterministic state machine (ingest → analyze → build tree → self-review → output), best for reliability, checkpoints, auditability. - CrewAI: multi-agent (Requirements-Analyst + Tree-Builder + Reviewer), fastest to a working multi-agent prototype. - Pydantic structured output for a guaranteed tree schema; run via CLI/cron/FastAPI. - Pros: max control, testable, portable. Cons: most code. ### Option D — OpenClaw - Always-on local agent; write a custom "problem-tree" skill, trigger it from chat (WhatsApp/Telegram/Slack), it reads the Doc and replies/writes the tree. - Pros: truly autonomous + chat-driven + broad system access. Cons: heavier setup, security/sandbox considerations, overkill for a focused task. ### Option E — No-code RAG platforms (Dify / Flowise) - Visual agent builders with built-in RAG; good if the doc(s) are large or you'll process many. - Pros: beginner-friendly, built-in vector store. Cons: another platform; RAG is unnecessary for a single short doc. My recommendation: Option A for the fastest result inside Cursor, with an easy upgrade path to Option C if you later want a standalone autonomous service. Option B is the sweet spot if you specifically want scheduled, no-code autonomy. A few quick decisions will let me write the concrete build plan. input.plan:
# Cursor-Native Requirement Analysis & Problem Tree Agent

## Goal
Drop a problem statement (with input/output constraints) into a local file, run one command/skill, and get back a thorough requirement analysis and a problem tree in Markdown + Mermaid + JSON.

## Why this approach
- Local file input means zero auth/infra (no Google Docs MCP/API needed).
- Runs on a frontier model directly for the best analysis quality with minimal plumbing.
- Output quality comes from a structured multi-pass pipeline, which is encoded into a reusable Cursor Skill.

## Workspace layout (to be created in `d:\ps2 tri`)
- `inputs/` - drop the problem statement file here (e.g. `inputs/problem.txt`)
- `outputs/` - generated artifacts per run (e.g. `outputs/<name>/analysis.md`, `tree.md`, `tree.json`)
- `.cursor/skills/problem-tree/SKILL.md` - the reusable agent definition
- `schema/problem-tree.schema.json` - the JSON schema for the structured output
- `README.md` - how to use it

## The agent pipeline (multi-pass, this is what drives output quality)
1. Ingest: read the input file from `inputs/`.
2. Extract: split into sections - problem statement, inputs, outputs, constraints, examples.
3. Requirement analysis: functional requirements, non-functional requirements, explicit input constraints, output constraints/format, assumptions, ambiguities, edge cases.
4. Problem tree: hierarchical decomposition - root problem -> sub-problems -> requirements -> constraints/inputs/outputs -> edge cases.
5. Self-critique pass: check completeness and consistency against the stated constraints; revise.
6. Emit: write `analysis.md`, `tree.md` (Mermaid), and `tree.json` (validated against the schema).

## Problem tree shape (Mermaid example)
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Core Problem] --> SP1[Sub-problem 1]
    Root --> SP2[Sub-problem 2]
    SP1 --> R1[Requirement]
    R1 --> C1[Input constraint]
    R1 --> O1[Output constraint]
    R1 --> E1[Edge case]
```

## Deliverables
- A working Cursor Skill that, given a file in `inputs/`, generates the three output artifacts.
- A JSON schema so the structured output is consistent and machine-readable.
- A sample run on a provided/sample problem statement to validate the pipeline end to end.

## Autonomy options (after core works)
- Cursor Automation or hook to auto-run when a new file lands in `inputs/`.
- The `/loop` skill for recurring runs.

## Upgrade path (only if unattended/headless runs are needed later)
- Port the same prompts/schema into a small Python LangGraph pipeline (ingest -> analyze -> tree -> self-review -> emit) runnable via CLI/cron/FastAPI. Documented, not built now.

## Open inputs needed from you at build time
- The problem statement file (or paste the content so I can save it to `inputs/`).
- Confirm output set: Markdown + Mermaid + JSON (default), or a subset.

---
name: problem-tree
description: Reads a local problem-statement file (with input/output constraints) and appends a detailed requirement analysis and a problem tree (Markdown + Mermaid + embedded JSON) back into the SAME file. Use when the user asks to analyze requirements, decompose a problem, or generate a problem tree from a file in inputs/ or any pasted problem statement.
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# Problem Tree Agent

Turn a raw problem statement into a thorough requirement analysis and a hierarchical problem tree, written back into the same file below the original content.

## When to run

The user names this skill and points at a target file (default: a file under `inputs/`). If no file is given, ask which file, or offer to save pasted text to `inputs/`.

## Pipeline (run every step, in order)

Copy this checklist and track progress:

```
- [ ] 1. Ingest: read the full target file
- [ ] 2. Extract: identify problem statement, inputs, outputs, constraints, examples
- [ ] 3. Analyze: derive requirements, assumptions, ambiguities, edge cases
- [ ] 4. Build tree: decompose root -> sub-problems -> requirements -> constraints/edge cases
- [ ] 5. Self-critique: check completeness/consistency vs stated constraints; revise
- [ ] 6. Append in place: write the section between delimiters into the same file
```

### 1. Ingest
Read the entire target file. Preserve its original content exactly; never edit anything above the generated section.

### 2. Extract
Separate the statement into: core problem, input spec, output spec, explicit constraints (ranges/sizes/formats), and any worked examples. If a part is missing, note it as an ambiguity rather than inventing it.

### 3. Analyze

- `root`: the core problem
- `subProblem`: the major parts to solve

### 5. Self-critique

- Every stated input/output constraint appears in the analysis and tree.
- No requirement contradicts a constraint.
- Edge cases cover the stated limits (min/max/empty/sentinel).
- Each tree node id is unique and Mermaid-safe (alphanumeric/underscore, no spaces).

input.contents:
Problem: Minimum Delivery Cost

You operate deliveries for an online store. There are N delivery zones,
numbered 1..N, connected by M bidirectional roads. Each road connects two
zones u and v with a positive travel cost w. The warehouse is at zone 1.

On a given day you must deliver to a set of K target zones. A single courier
starts at the warehouse and may pass through any zones/roads any number of
times. Report the minimum total travel cost required so that every target
zone is visited at least once. The courier does not need to return to the
warehouse.

Input:
- Line 1: three integers N M K
- Next M lines: three integers u v w describing a road
- Last line: K integers, the target zones

Output:
- A single integer: the minimum total travel cost.
- Print -1 if at least one target zone is unreachable from the warehouse.

Constraints:
- 1 <= N <= 100000
- 0 <= M <= 200000
- 1 <= K <= N
- 1 <= u, v <= N, u != v
- 1 <= w <= 1000000000
- Target zones are distinct.

Example:
Input:
4 4 2
1 2 3
2 3 1
1 3 7
3 4 2
3 4
Output:
6

# Problem Tree Agent

A Cursor-native autonomous agent that reads a local problem-statement file (problem
description plus input/output constraints) and appends a detailed **requirement
analysis** and a **problem tree** back into that same file.

No Google Docs API, OAuth, or external service is required: paste your Google Doc
content into a local file and run the agent.

## How to use

1. Put your problem statement into a file under `inputs/` (any extension works,
   e.g. `inputs/problem.txt`). Paste the content directly from your Google Doc.
2. In Cursor chat, invoke the skill by name and point it at the file, for example:

   ```
   /problem-tree analyze inputs/problem.txt
   ```

   (or just: "Run the problem-tree skill on `inputs/problem.txt`")
3. The agent runs a multi-pass pipeline and appends the result into the same file,
   between `<!-- PROBLEM-TREE:START -->` and `<!-- PROBLEM-TREE:END -->`.

Re-running on the same file replaces the generated section in place (no
duplication). Your original content above the start marker is never modified.

## What gets appended

- A one-paragraph summary of the core problem.
- Requirements: functional, non-functional, input constraints, output constraints,
  assumptions, ambiguities, and edge cases.
- A Mermaid problem tree (root problem -> sub-problems -> requirements / constraints
  / edge cases).
- A machine-readable JSON block validating against `schema/problem-tree.schema.json`.

## Project layout

- `inputs/` — problem-statement files (input and output live in the same file).
- `.cursor/skills/problem-tree/SKILL.md` — the agent definition (the pipeline).
- `schema/problem-tree.schema.json` — JSON schema for the structured output.

## The pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ingest file] --> B[Extract sections]
    B --> C[Requirement analysis]
    C --> D[Build problem tree]
    D --> E[Self-critique pass]
    E --> F[Append in place]
```

## Making it autonomous (optional)

- Cursor Automations or a hook can run the skill automatically when a new file
  lands in `inputs/`.
- The `/loop` skill can run it on a recurring schedule.

## Upgrade path: headless / unattended runs

If you later need this to run outside Cursor (server, cron, CI), port the same
prompts and `schema/problem-tree.schema.json` into a small Python
[LangGraph](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/) pipeline with nodes:
`ingest -> analyze -> build_tree -> self_review -> append`. Use a Pydantic model
mirroring the JSON schema for validated output, and expose it via CLI, cron, or a
FastAPI endpoint. This keeps the analysis logic identical while removing the
dependency on the Cursor UI.

# Requirement Analysis & Problem Tree

> Generated by the problem-tree skill. Original problem statement is above this line.

## Summary
A single courier starts at the warehouse (zone 1) and must travel along
bidirectional, reusable roads so that every one of the K target zones is visited at
least once, while minimizing the total traversed cost; the courier need not return
to the warehouse. The program reads the weighted graph and target set and prints the
minimum total cost, or -1 if any target is unreachable from zone 1.

## Requirements

### Functional
- **FR1** — Parse N, M, K, the M weighted bidirectional roads, and the K target zones. _(must)_
- **FR2** — Determine which target zones are reachable from zone 1; if any is unreachable, output -1. _(must)_
- **FR3** — Compute the minimum total travel cost of a single walk from zone 1 that visits every target at least once, edges reusable. _(must)_
- **FR4** — Print exactly one integer: the minimum cost, or -1. _(must)_

### Non-functional
- **NFR1** — Scale to N <= 100000 and M <= 200000 within typical time limits (graph algorithms near O((N+M) log N)). _(must)_
- **NFR2** — Use 64-bit integers: a minimal walk can sum to ~1e14 ((N-1) * max w), which overflows 32-bit. _(must)_
- **NFR3** — Memory linear in N + M (adjacency list, not matrix; matrix is 1e10 cells). _(must)_

### Input constraints
- **N (zones)** — 1 <= N <= 100000.
- **M (roads)** — 0 <= M <= 200000.
- **K (targets)** — 1 <= K <= N.
- **u, v (endpoints)** — 1 <= u, v <= N, u != v (no self-loops).
- **w (cost)** — 1 <= w <= 1000000000 (strictly positive).
- **targets** — K distinct zone ids.

### Output constraints
- **Value** — a single integer, the minimum total travel cost.
- **Unreachable** — print -1 if at least one target is unreachable from zone 1.
- **No return** — cost excludes any return trip to the warehouse.

### Assumptions
- The graph may be disconnected; M = 0 is valid.
- Parallel roads between the same pair of zones are allowed; keep the minimum-cost one.
- Targets may include zone 1 itself, which contributes 0 to the cost.
- Costs are positive, so no negative-cycle handling is needed (Dijkstra-class is valid).

### Ambiguities / open questions
- The dominant ambiguity: FR3 as stated ("one courier visits all targets in a single
  journey") is the path/Steiner-walk version of TSP, which is NP-hard for general K
  and is inconsistent with K <= N = 1e5. Two plausible intended readings:
  (a) a single connected walk visiting all targets (matches the example answer 6, but
  intractable at scale), or (b) the sum of independent shortest paths from zone 1 to
  each target (tractable, but yields 10 on the example, not 6). The example favors
  reading (a); the constraints favor reading (b). This must be clarified before
  implementation.
- Are multiple couriers allowed? Statement says "a single courier"; assumed exactly one.
- Output for an empty target set is undefined, but K >= 1 rules it out.

### Edge cases
- K = 1: a single target.
- Target set includes zone 1: that target costs 0.
- M = 0 with a target other than zone 1: unreachable -> -1.
- A target equal to zone 1 only, with N = 1: answer 0.
- Parallel edges and large weights causing 32-bit overflow.
- All targets reachable but graph otherwise disconnected (ignore unreachable non-target zones).

## Problem tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Min-cost courier walk from warehouse visiting all K targets"]
    Root --> SP1["Parse and build weighted graph"]
    Root --> SP2["Determine target reachability from zone 1"]
    Root --> SP3["Compute min-cost walk visiting all targets"]
    Root --> SP4["Emit result: integer cost or -1"]

    SP1 --> IC_N["input: 1 <= N <= 1e5"]
    SP1 --> IC_M["input: 0 <= M <= 2e5"]
    SP1 --> IC_W["input: 1 <= w <= 1e9"]
    SP1 --> EC_multi["edge: parallel roads -> keep min"]

    SP2 --> FR2node["FR2: detect unreachable targets"]
    SP2 --> OC_neg1["output: print -1 if any target unreachable"]
    SP2 --> EC_disc["edge: disconnected graph / M=0"]

    SP3 --> FR3node["FR3: minimize total cost, edges reusable"]
    SP3 --> NFR1node["NFR1: handle N=1e5, M=2e5"]
    SP3 --> AMB_model["ambiguity: single walk (TSP/Steiner, NP-hard) vs sum of shortest paths"]
    SP3 --> EC_self["edge: target includes warehouse -> 0"]

    SP4 --> OC_int["output: single integer"]
    SP4 --> NFR2node["NFR2: 64-bit to avoid overflow (~1e14)"]
    SP4 --> EC_single["edge: K=1"]
```

# Requirement Analysis & Problem Tree

> Generated by the problem-tree skill. Original problem statement is above this line.

## Summary
The goal is a Cursor-scoped tool that observes a user's conversations with the
Cursor agent and gives timely feedback and coaching on the quality of their
prompting and questioning. For each user prompt it judges whether the prompt
delivers insight (vs merely requesting information), labels it as great or not,
explains the reasoning, and proposes a better alternative when the prompt scores
poorly. The intent is to help people learn the high-value behaviors (asking the
right questions, supplying context, cross-questioning to mitigate risk, clear
articulation, recommendation attitude, bias for action) faster than learning by
trial and error.

## Requirements

### Functional
- **FR1** — Ingest per-conversation data: user messages to the agent, Cursor responses, and timestamps. _(must)_
- **FR2** — Evaluate each user prompt and classify its insight quality (great vs not great). _(must)_
- **FR3** — Produce the reasoning behind each classification. _(must)_
- **FR4** — When a prompt scores negatively, suggest a better alternative prompt. _(must)_
- **FR5** — Deliver the feedback/coaching back to the user. _(must)_
- **FR6** — Assess prompts against the named good practices (right questions, context/insight, cross-questioning, clear articulation, recommendation attitude, bias for action). _(should)_

### Non-functional
- **NFR1** — Timely feedback: latency/cadence fast enough to support learning (real-time per prompt or near it). _(must)_
- **NFR2** — Scope limited to Cursor conversations only; workspace content and cross-tool observation are explicitly out for v1. _(must)_
- **NFR3** — Built for incremental value: core must stand alone so other surfaces can be layered on later. _(should)_
- **NFR4** — Privacy: data already flows to an LLM via Cursor, so no extra exposure for cloud models; revisit if a local LLM is used. _(should)_

### Input constraints
- **User utterances** — what the user says to the agent.
- **Cursor responses** — the agent's replies.
- **Timestamp** — per message, to reason about timing/cadence.
- **Workspace content** — scoped out for v1 simplification (not consumed).

### Output constraints
- **Per-prompt insight label** — each prompt segregated as great or not.
- **Reasoning** — explanation supporting each insight/label.
- **Alternative suggestion** — a better prompt offered only when the insight is negative.

### Assumptions
- A "prompt" is a single user message; evaluation is primarily per-prompt.
- Quality is judged against the explicitly listed good practices.
- Feedback via an LLM is acceptable because Cursor already routes data to an LLM (unless a local LLM is in use).
- v1 evaluates conversation only, not code/workspace artifacts.

### Ambiguities / open questions
- Sourcing of input data: how are prompts/responses/timestamps captured (Cursor hook, logs, API, extension)?
- Engine making good decisions: what model and rubric define great vs not, and how is judgment reliability ensured/validated?
- Feedback delivery: when, how long, in what form, and what content (the statement leaves all four open).
- "Insight vs information" must be operationalized into a measurable rubric.
- "Timely" is unquantified (per prompt, end of session, or periodic?).
- Granularity: per-prompt only, or also per-session/longitudinal trends and learning over time?

### Edge cases
- Trivial or one-word prompts with little to evaluate.
- Prompts that are commands/statements rather than questions.
- Multi-turn prompts only meaningful with prior context.
- Local-LLM scenario where privacy expectations differ.
- High prompt volume causing feedback noise/fatigue.
- A session with no negative prompts (no alternatives to suggest).
- Non-English prompts.

## Problem tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Cursor prompt-quality feedback and coaching tool"]
    Root --> SP1["Capture conversation data"]
    Root --> SP2["Evaluate prompt insight quality"]
    Root --> SP3["Generate reasoning and alternatives"]
    Root --> SP4["Deliver feedback and coaching"]
    Root --> SP5["Scope and privacy constraints"]

    SP1 --> IC_user["input: user utterances to agent"]
    SP1 --> IC_resp["input: Cursor responses"]
    SP1 --> IC_ts["input: timestamp"]
    SP1 --> AMB_source["ambiguity: how is data sourced?"]
    SP1 --> AS_ws["scoped out: workspace content"]

    SP2 --> FR2node["FR2: classify great vs not"]
    SP2 --> AMB_engine["ambiguity: rubric and engine reliability"]
    SP2 --> EC_trivial["edge: trivial / non-question prompts"]
    SP2 --> EC_context["edge: multi-turn context-dependent prompt"]

    SP3 --> FR3node["FR3: reasoning per insight"]
    SP3 --> OC_alt["output: alternative only when negative"]
    SP3 --> EC_noneg["edge: no negative prompts in session"]

    SP4 --> FR5node["FR5: deliver feedback to user"]
    SP4 --> NFR1node["NFR1: timely (when/how-long/form/content)"]
    SP4 --> EC_volume["edge: high volume -> feedback fatigue"]

    SP5 --> NFR2node["NFR2: Cursor conversation only (v1)"]
    SP5 --> NFR4node["NFR4: privacy ok unless local LLM"]
    SP5 --> NFR3node["NFR3: incremental, extensible core"]
```

## Making it autonomous (optional)

Cursor Automations are schedule/event triggered (cron, git, Slack, etc.); there is
no native "watch this folder" trigger. The practical recipe is a scheduled
automation that scans `inputs/` and processes anything not yet analyzed.

with an hourly/daily cron trigger and this prompt:

```
Run the problem-tree skill on every file in inputs/ that does NOT already
contain a "<!-- PROBLEM-TREE:START -->" marker. For each such file, append the
requirement analysis and problem tree in place per the skill. Skip files that
already have the marker. Report which files you processed.
```

This makes new files dropped into `inputs/` get analyzed on the next run.

### Recipe B — on demand within a session

Use the `/loop` skill to re-run the analysis on a recurring interval inside an
active chat session, e.g. `/loop 30m run the problem-tree skill on new inputs/ files`.

### Recipe C — fully unattended (no Cursor UI)

Use the LangGraph upgrade path below plus an OS scheduler (Windows Task Scheduler
or cron) to watch `inputs/` and run the pipeline headlessly.

### 1. Ingest
Read the entire target file. Preserve its original content exactly; never edit anything above the generated section.

### 2. Extract
Separate the statement into: core problem, input spec, output spec, explicit constraints (ranges/sizes/formats), and any worked examples. If a part is missing, note it as an ambiguity rather than inventing it.

### 3. Analyze
Produce:
- Functional requirements (what it must do), ids `FR1..`
- Non-functional requirements (time/space limits, scalability, reliability), ids `NFR1..`
- Input constraints (verbatim ranges/limits, normalized)
- Output constraints (format, ordering, precision, special/sentinel values)
- Assumptions (only where the statement is silent)
- Ambiguities (anything underspecified or contradictory)
- Edge cases (boundaries: empty, min, max, duplicates, disconnected, overflow, etc.)

### 4. Build tree
Decompose into a tree keyed by node ids:
- `root`: the core problem
- `subProblem`: the major parts to solve
- `requirement`, `inputConstraint`, `outputConstraint`, `edgeCase`, `assumption`: leaves under the relevant sub-problem

### 5. Self-critique
Before writing, verify:
- Every stated input/output constraint appears in the analysis and tree.
- No requirement contradicts a constraint.
- Edge cases cover the stated limits (min/max/empty/sentinel).
- Each tree node id is unique and Mermaid-safe (alphanumeric/underscore, no spaces).
Fix gaps, then continue.

### 6. Append in place
Build the section using the template below and write it into the SAME file:
- If the file has no `<!-- PROBLEM-TREE:START -->` marker: append the section (preceded by one blank line) to the end of the file.
- If the markers already exist: replace everything between `<!-- PROBLEM-TREE:START -->` and `<!-- PROBLEM-TREE:END -->` (inclusive) with the new section. Do not duplicate.

I did deep research (LLM-as-judge reliability, feedback/learning science, questioning rubrics, nudge/adoption). Here's the senior-analyst problem breakdown, run through the two prioritization iterations you asked for.

## Iteration 1 — extract everything, prioritize, cut to top 6

Full candidate set surfaced from the document (deep read):

1. Operationalizing "insight vs information" into a real rubric
2. Reliability of the evaluation engine (LLM grading prompts)
3. Context paradox: judging "the right question at the right time" while workspace content is scoped out
4. Feedback design — when / how long / what form / what content
5. Data sourcing/capture inside Cursor
6. Proving it actually improves skill (success metric)
7. Quality of "better alternative" suggestions
8. Privacy / consent / trust (doc waves this away)
9. Adoption & behavior change / reactance to being judged
10. Personalization, cold start, skill level, work vs study
11. Coaching tone & psychological safety
12. Scope discipline / incremental rollout

Sorted by priority (criticality x dependency x risk x validity-threat) → **top 6 kept**, the rest removed:

| # | Point | Why it ranks |
|---|-------|--------------|
| 1 | Quality rubric (#1) | Foundational — everything downstream is meaningless without a valid definition of "great" |
| 2 | Context paradox (#3) | Threatens validity of *every* judgment; a non-obvious structural flaw |
| 3 | Engine reliability (#2) | The "engine making good decisions" open question; high, documented risk |
| 4 | Feedback design (#4) | The doc's own four open questions; core to "coaching" |
| 5 | Efficacy metric (#6) | Without it you can't tell if the tool works at all |
| 6 | Data capture (#5) | Hard gate — nothing runs if you can't reliably get the data |

Removed: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12.

## Iteration 2 — very deep research on the survivors, re-prioritize and merge, final top 6

In this pass I deepened each survivor with external evidence and folded the strongest removed items (trust, adoption, tone) into them, then re-ranked.

### 1. The rubric *is* the product — operationalize "insight vs information"
The document's thesis ("value = insight, not information") is currently a slogan, not a measurable construct. This is the foundation; if "great vs not" isn't defined rigorously, every later score is arbitrary. The mature analogue already exists in education: [Bloom's taxonomy and questioning research](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3776909/) distinguishes lower-order (recall/convergent) from higher-order (analyze/evaluate/create, divergent) questions, with explicit action verbs and performance levels. Recommendation: define 4-6 weighted criteria (e.g., specificity, supplies context/recommendation, surfaces risk via cross-questioning, advances the goal) with concrete level descriptors — not a binary. The binary "great or not" in the doc is itself a design smell.

### 2. The context paradox — the sharpest flaw
The doc says value is "asking the right question *at the right time*," yet INPUT explicitly **scopes out workspace content**. You cannot judge whether a question was the right one for the task without knowing the task. Judging prompts in isolation will systematically misfire (good prompts in a rich context look "vague," and vice versa). This is a direct internal contradiction between the value definition and the scope. Recommendation: keep v1 scope but evaluate on the *conversation thread* (prompt + Cursor responses + sequence), and be explicit that goal-alignment judgments are deferred until workspace context is in scope — don't claim "right question at the right time" until then.

### 3. A trustworthy evaluation engine (LLM-as-judge)
"Engine making good decisions" is the highest-risk unknown. The evidence is blunt: LLM judges carry [leniency, verbosity, position, and self-preference biases](https://arize.com/blog/judging-the-judges-llm-as-a-judge/), can violate transitivity, and [must be calibrated against human ground truth](https://www.langchain.com/resources/llm-as-a-judge) rather than trusted raw. Practical bar: build a golden set of ~100-300 human-labeled prompts and measure agreement with [Cohen's kappa](https://github.com/aws-samples/sample-GEDD/blob/main/grounded-evals/docs/cohens-kappa-for-llm-judges.md) (κ≥0.8 for autonomous use; 0.4-0.6 means human-in-the-loop only), use a cross-family model as judge, add length penalties, and recalibrate quarterly. Without this, the product ships confident noise.

### 4. Feedback as an intervention, not a verdict (timing, frequency, form, tone)
This merges the doc's four open questions with fatigue/tone. Learning science is counter-intuitive here: immediate feedback speeds early acquisition but [too-frequent feedback harms long-term retention](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/pdfs/ADA328695.pdf) — the "guidance hypothesis" / inverted-U, with ~50% (faded over time) outperforming 100%. Combined with [alert/nudge fatigue](https://dedhamgroup.com/ai-and-the-next-wave-of-alert-fatigue-in-oncology/) (relevance over volume, context-aware timing, frequency caps), the answer to "when/how often" is: not every prompt. Recommendation: batched, faded, self-controllable feedback (let users pull detail) delivered at natural breakpoints — and framed as coaching, since being graded triggers reactance.

### 5. Proving it works — efficacy and adoption, not vanity metrics
The doc never defines success. Research on behavior-change tooling warns that [nudges are delivery tools, not diagnostic ones](https://seramount.com/articles/why-nudge-tech-alone-doesnt-drive-real-behavior-change/) and that [logins/message counts tell you nothing](https://www.heypinnacle.com/blog/ai-implementation-playbook) — track behavior change at the moments that matter, via A/B tests. Recommendation: define a learning-outcome metric up front (e.g., measured rise in prompt-quality scores over N sessions, acceptance rate of suggested alternatives), and instrument from day one. This is the lens that separates a demo from a product.

### 6. Data capture and consent — the enabling gate
Two issues collapse into one prerequisite. Technical: reliably sourcing prompts + Cursor responses + timestamps (the doc's "sourcing of input data" question) is unresolved and gates everything. Trust: the doc dismisses privacy ("already sent to an LLM"), but you are now *storing, scoring, and coaching a person on their own behavior*, and the [local-LLM case breaks the assumption](https://www.heypinnacle.com/blog/ai-implementation-playbook); launching without a clear privacy/purpose story fuels fear. Recommendation: settle the capture mechanism first, and pair it with explicit consent and a "what we store and why" story.

I've also appended this final top-6 breakdown to your document (a new section below the problem tree, leaving the tree intact). Let me add that now.

I'm working through a structured analysis here—starting with comprehensive research to identify all the important factors, then running two rounds of prioritization to narrow down to the top six issues. First I'll compile the full candidate list, then apply iteration one to cut it down, and finally do a deeper dive in iteration two to arrive at the final ranked set.

The prioritization framework I'm using focuses on criticality to core value, whether something is foundational or unblocks other work, the risk and uncertainty involved, and whether it threatens the product's validity. Looking at the candidates: there's the foundational need for an explicit rubric defining "insight vs information" grounded in Bloom's taxonomy—without validated criteria, quality judgments become arbitrary. Then there's the major risk around LLM-as-judge reliability, since research shows these models have systematic biases like leniency and position bias that could make judgments unreliable without calibration against human ground truth. There's also a critical internal contradiction: the evaluation requires understanding whether a question was right for the user's context and goals, but the scope excludes workspace content, removing the very context needed to make that judgment. And the feedback design has open questions around timing, frequency, and form—learning science suggests immediate feedback helps acquisition but too-frequent feedback harms retention, with an optimal fade-over-time pattern.

Beyond those, I'm tracking data sourcing feasibility in Cursor as a technical dependency, whether coaching actually improves skill as a success metric (nudges alone don't drive behavior change without measurement), the quality of alternative suggestions generated, privacy and consent concerns that the doc glosses over, adoption and whether users will actually act on feedback, and personalization challenges across different skill levels. There's also the psychological safety aspect—poorly constructed feedback stifles learning. I'm now sorting these by priority to identify the top six that are truly blocking.

The ranking that emerges: the rubric is foundational since everything depends on it; context validity and scope contradictions threaten the validity of every judgment; judge reliability is the engine with high risk; feedback design is explicitly open in the doc and grounded in science; efficacy and success metrics are essential to know if this works at all; and data capture feasibility is a gating dependency. - Engine making good decisions
  - Build a **calibrated judgment engine** that can explain prompt-quality decisions reliably, instead of merely producing confident-sounding opinions.

  - Define “good decision” before choosing the model
    - Convert the product philosophy into explicit judgement criteria: insight quality, context usefulness, risk awareness, clarity, recommendation attitude, and bias for action.

  - Use a rubric, not a vague classifier
    - Score prompts across a small set of dimensions rather than only labeling them as “great” or “not great,” because binary judgment hides why the prompt failed.

  - Calibrate against human-labelled examples
    - Create a gold set of real Cursor conversations labelled by humans, then compare the engine’s judgment against those labels before trusting it.

  - Separate reasoning quality from writing polish
    - Prevent the engine from rewarding prompts that sound articulate but still offload thinking, lack context, or fail to move the task forward.

  - Judge with conversation context, not the latest prompt alone
    - Include the previous turns and Cursor response so the engine can evaluate whether the user asked the right follow-up at the right moment.

  - Detect uncertainty and defer judgment
    - When context is missing or the prompt is ambiguous, the engine should say “insufficient context” instead of forcing a confident score.

  - Review disagreement patterns
    - Track where the engine and human reviewers disagree, then refine the rubric or examples rather than blindly adjusting model prompts.

  - Protect against LLM judge bias
    - Control for verbosity bias, leniency bias, and style preference so longer or more polished prompts are not automatically judged better.

  - Generate coaching, not just scores
    - Every negative judgment should map to a specific improvement path: what was missing, why it mattered, and what a stronger prompt would look like.

  - Continuously monitor drift
    - Re-test the engine on fresh labelled examples over time because model behavior, user behavior, and product expectations will change.

# Creating Skills in Cursor

This skill guides you through creating effective Agent Skills for Cursor. Skills are markdown files that teach the agent how to perform specific tasks: reviewing PRs using team standards, generating commit messages in a preferred format, querying database schemas, or any specialized workflow.

## Before You Begin: Gather Requirements

Before creating a skill, gather essential information from the user about:

1. **Purpose and scope**: What specific task or workflow should this skill help with?
2. **Target location**: Should this be a personal skill (~/.cursor/skills/) or project skill (.cursor/skills/)?
3. **Trigger scenarios**: When should the agent automatically apply this skill?
4. **Key domain knowledge**: What specialized information does the agent need that it wouldn't already know?
5. **Output format preferences**: Are there specific templates, formats, or styles required?
6. **Existing patterns**: Are there existing examples or conventions to follow?

### Verbatim text from the user

If the user includes exact wording to use in the skill, respect it and use it **verbatim** in `SKILL.md` (same words, same order). Do not paraphrase, soften, or expand their copy, and do not add unrequested headings or commentary around it.

### Inferring from Context

If you have previous conversation context, infer the skill from what was discussed. You can create skills based on workflows, patterns, or domain knowledge that emerged in the conversation.

### Gathering Additional Information

If you need clarification, use the AskQuestion tool when available:

```
Example AskQuestion usage:
- "Where should this skill be stored?" with options like ["Personal (~/.cursor/skills/)", "Project (.cursor/skills/)"]
- "Should this skill include executable scripts?" with options like ["Yes", "No"]
```

If the AskQuestion tool is not available, ask these questions conversationally.

---

## Skill File Structure

### Directory Layout

Skills are stored as directories containing a `SKILL.md` file:

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md              # Required - main instructions
├── reference.md          # Optional - detailed documentation
├── examples.md           # Optional - usage examples
└── scripts/              # Optional - utility scripts
    ├── validate.py
    └── helper.sh
```

### Storage Locations

| Type | Path | Scope |
|------|------|-------|
| Personal | ~/.cursor/skills/skill-name/ | Available across all your projects |
| Project | .cursor/skills/skill-name/ | Shared with anyone using the repository |

**IMPORTANT**: Never create skills in `~/.cursor/skills-cursor/`. This directory is reserved for Cursor's internal built-in skills and is managed automatically by the system.

### SKILL.md Structure

Every skill requires a `SKILL.md` file with YAML frontmatter and markdown body:

```markdown
---
name: your-skill-name
description: Brief description of what this skill does and when to use it
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# Your Skill Name

## Instructions
Clear, step-by-step guidance for the agent.

## Examples
Concrete examples of using this skill.
```

Default `disable-model-invocation: true` so the skill only loads when named explicitly. Omit it only when the agent should auto-invoke from ambient context.

### Required Metadata Fields

| Field | Requirements | Purpose |
|-------|--------------|---------|
| `name` | Max 64 chars, lowercase letters/numbers/hyphens only | Unique identifier for the skill |
| `description` | Max 1024 chars, non-empty | Helps agent decide when to apply the skill |

---

## Writing Effective Descriptions

The description is **critical** for skill discovery. The agent uses it to decide when to apply your skill.

### Description Best Practices

1. **Write in third person** (the description is injected into the system prompt):
   - ✅ Good: "Processes Excel files and generates reports"
   - ❌ Avoid: "I can help you process Excel files"
   - ❌ Avoid: "You can use this to process Excel files"

2. **Be specific and include trigger terms**:
   - ✅ Good: "Extract text and tables from PDF files, fill forms, merge documents. Use when working with PDF files or when the user mentions PDFs, forms, or document extraction."
   - ❌ Vague: "Helps with documents"

3. **Include both WHAT and WHEN**:
   - WHAT: What the skill does (specific capabilities)
   - WHEN: When the agent should use it (trigger scenarios)

### Description Examples

```yaml
# PDF Processing
description: Extract text and tables from PDF files, fill forms, merge documents. Use when working with PDF files or when the user mentions PDFs, forms, or document extraction.

# Excel Analysis
description: Analyze Excel spreadsheets, create pivot tables, generate charts. Use when analyzing Excel files, spreadsheets, tabular data, or .xlsx files.

# Git Commit Helper
description: Generate descriptive commit messages by analyzing git diffs. Use when the user asks for help writing commit messages or reviewing staged changes.

# Code Review
description: Review code for quality, security, and best practices following team standards. Use when reviewing pull requests, code changes, or when the user asks for a code review.
```

---

## Core Authoring Principles

### 1. Concise is Key

The context window is shared with conversation history, other skills, and requests. Every token competes for space.

**Default assumption**: The agent is already very smart. Only add context it doesn't already have.

Challenge each piece of information:
- "Does the agent really need this explanation?"
- "Can I assume the agent knows this?"
- "Does this paragraph justify its token cost?"

**Good (concise)**:
```markdown
## Extract PDF text

Use pdfplumber for text extraction:

\`\`\`python
import pdfplumber

with pdfplumber.open("file.pdf") as pdf:
    text = pdf.pages[0].extract_text()
\`\`\`
```

**Bad (verbose)**:
```markdown
## Extract PDF text

PDF (Portable Document Format) files are a common file format that contains
text, images, and other content. To extract text from a PDF, you'll need to
use a library. There are many libraries available for PDF processing, but we
recommend pdfplumber because it's easy to use and handles most cases well...
```

### 2. Keep SKILL.md Under 500 Lines

For optimal performance, the main SKILL.md file should be concise. Use progressive disclosure for detailed content.

### 3. Progressive Disclosure

Put essential information in SKILL.md; detailed reference material in separate files that the agent reads only when needed.

```markdown
# PDF Processing

## Quick start
[Essential instructions here]

## Additional resources
- For complete API details, see [reference.md](reference.md)
- For usage examples, see [examples.md](examples.md)
```

**Keep references one level deep** - link directly from SKILL.md to reference files. Deeply nested references may result in partial reads.

### 4. Set Appropriate Degrees of Freedom

Match specificity to the task's fragility:

| Freedom Level | When to Use | Example |
|---------------|-------------|---------|
| **High** (text instructions) | Multiple valid approaches, context-dependent | Code review guidelines |
| **Medium** (pseudocode/templates) | Preferred pattern with acceptable variation | Report generation |
| **Low** (specific scripts) | Fragile operations, consistency critical | Database migrations |

---

## Common Patterns

### Template Pattern

Provide output format templates:

```markdown
## Report structure

Use this template:

\`\`\`markdown
# [Analysis Title]

## Executive summary
[One-paragraph overview of key findings]

## Key findings
- Finding 1 with supporting data
- Finding 2 with supporting data

## Recommendations
1. Specific actionable recommendation
2. Specific actionable recommendation
\`\`\`
```

### Examples Pattern

For skills where output quality depends on seeing examples:

```markdown
## Commit message format

**Example 1:**
Input: Added user authentication with JWT tokens
Output:
\`\`\`
feat(auth): implement JWT-based authentication

Add login endpoint and token validation middleware
\`\`\`

**Example 2:**
Input: Fixed bug where dates displayed incorrectly
Output:
\`\`\`
fix(reports): correct date formatting in timezone conversion

Use UTC timestamps consistently across report generation
\`\`\`
```

### Workflow Pattern

Break complex operations into clear steps with checklists:

```markdown
## Form filling workflow

Copy this checklist and track progress:

\`\`\`
Task Progress:
- [ ] Step 1: Analyze the form
- [ ] Step 2: Create field mapping
- [ ] Step 3: Validate mapping
- [ ] Step 4: Fill the form
- [ ] Step 5: Verify output
\`\`\`

**Step 1: Analyze the form**
Run: \`python scripts/analyze_form.py input.pdf\`
...
```

### Conditional Workflow Pattern

Guide through decision points:

```markdown
## Document modification workflow

1. Determine the modification type:

   **Creating new content?** → Follow "Creation workflow" below
   **Editing existing content?** → Follow "Editing workflow" below

2. Creation workflow:
   - Use docx-js library
   - Build document from scratch
   ...
```

### Feedback Loop Pattern

For quality-critical tasks, implement validation loops:

```markdown
## Document editing process

1. Make your edits
2. **Validate immediately**: \`python scripts/validate.py output/\`
3. If validation fails:
   - Review the error message
   - Fix the issues
   - Run validation again
4. **Only proceed when validation passes**
```

---

## Utility Scripts

Pre-made scripts offer advantages over generated code:
- More reliable than generated code
- Save tokens (no code in context)
- Save time (no code generation)
- Ensure consistency across uses

```markdown
## Utility scripts

**analyze_form.py**: Extract all form fields from PDF
\`\`\`bash
python scripts/analyze_form.py input.pdf > fields.json
\`\`\`

**validate.py**: Check for errors
\`\`\`bash
python scripts/validate.py fields.json
# Returns: "OK" or lists conflicts
\`\`\`
```

Make clear whether the agent should **execute** the script (most common) or **read** it as reference.

---

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid

### 1. Windows-Style Paths
- ✅ Use: `scripts/helper.py`
- ❌ Avoid: `scripts\helper.py`

### 2. Too Many Options
```markdown
# Bad - confusing
"You can use pypdf, or pdfplumber, or PyMuPDF, or..."

# Good - provide a default with escape hatch
"Use pdfplumber for text extraction.
For scanned PDFs requiring OCR, use pdf2image with pytesseract instead."
```

### 3. Time-Sensitive Information
```markdown
# Bad - will become outdated
"If you're doing this before August 2025, use the old API."

# Good - use an "old patterns" section
## Current method
Use the v2 API endpoint.

## Old patterns (deprecated)
<details>
<summary>Legacy v1 API</summary>
...
</details>
```

### 4. Inconsistent Terminology
Choose one term and use it throughout:
- ✅ Always "API endpoint" (not mixing "URL", "route", "path")
- ✅ Always "field" (not mixing "box", "element", "control")

### 5. Vague Skill Names
- ✅ Good: `processing-pdfs`, `analyzing-spreadsheets`
- ❌ Avoid: `helper`, `utils`, `tools`

---

## Skill Creation Workflow

When helping a user create a skill, follow this process:

### Phase 1: Discovery

Gather information about:
1. The skill's purpose and primary use case
2. Storage location (personal vs project)
3. Trigger scenarios
4. Any specific requirements or constraints
5. Existing examples or patterns to follow

If you have access to the AskQuestion tool, use it for efficient structured gathering. Otherwise, ask conversationally.

### Phase 2: Design

1. Draft the skill name (lowercase, hyphens, max 64 chars)
2. Write a specific, third-person description
3. Outline the main sections needed
4. Identify if supporting files or scripts are needed

### Phase 3: Implementation

1. Create the directory structure
2. Write the SKILL.md file with frontmatter
3. Create any supporting reference files
4. Create any utility scripts if needed

### Phase 4: Verification

1. Verify the SKILL.md is under 500 lines
2. Check that the description is specific and includes trigger terms
3. Ensure consistent terminology throughout
4. Verify all file references are one level deep
5. Test that the skill can be discovered and applied

---

## Complete Example

Here's a complete example of a well-structured skill:

**Directory structure:**
```
code-review/
├── SKILL.md
├── STANDARDS.md
└── examples.md
```

**SKILL.md:**
```markdown
---
name: code-review
description: Review code for quality, security, and maintainability following team standards. Use when reviewing pull requests, examining code changes, or when the user asks for a code review.
---

# Code Review

## Quick Start

When reviewing code:

1. Check for correctness and potential bugs
2. Verify security best practices
3. Assess code readability and maintainability
4. Ensure tests are adequate

## Review Checklist

- [ ] Logic is correct and handles edge cases
- [ ] No security vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, etc.)
- [ ] Code follows project style conventions
- [ ] Functions are appropriately sized and focused
- [ ] Error handling is comprehensive
- [ ] Tests cover the changes

## Providing Feedback

Format feedback as:
- 🔴 **Critical**: Must fix before merge
- 🟡 **Suggestion**: Consider improving
- 🟢 **Nice to have**: Optional enhancement

## Additional Resources

- For detailed coding standards, see [STANDARDS.md](STANDARDS.md)
- For example reviews, see [examples.md](examples.md)
```

---

## Summary Checklist

Before finalizing a skill, verify:

### Core Quality
- [ ] Description is specific and includes key terms
- [ ] Description includes both WHAT and WHEN
- [ ] Written in third person
- [ ] SKILL.md body is under 500 lines
- [ ] Consistent terminology throughout
- [ ] Examples are concrete, not abstract

### Structure
- [ ] File references are one level deep
- [ ] Progressive disclosure used appropriately
- [ ] Workflows have clear steps
- [ ] No time-sensitive information

### If Including Scripts
- [ ] Scripts solve problems rather than punt
- [ ] Required packages are documented
- [ ] Error handling is explicit and helpful
- [ ] No Windows-style paths

+- Describe what people currently do and why it is painful.
+- Name where information, effort, or decisions are fragmented.
